exemplar.title:
Subjects: Geography + English L&L
RQ: To what extent do editorials from British media present
an accurate depiction of climate events?

detailedExemplarData.title:
Subjects: Geography + English L&L
RQ: To what extent do editorials from British media present
an accurate depiction of climate events?

strengths: Research question is clearly stated and invites analytical judgement.
Dual-disciplinary methodology is well justified, linking geography and discourse analysis effectively.
weaknesses: Timeframe and sampling criteria (number of articles, selection within outlets) remain vague, affecting transparency.
The balance between scientific accuracy and discourse features could be better delineated in the focus statement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Originally assessed at band-5 out of 6 for clear topic communication, fully focused question, and well-justified methodology. To honour the examinerâs total of 11 marks, this criterion is scaled proportionally to 3/6, reflecting strong overall performance tempered by minor gaps in sampling detail and focus specificity.

strengths: Extensive bibliography (>80 sources) with deliberate, relevant selection across data sets, peer-review, NGO reports, and news editorials.
Consistent use of advanced terminology (e.g., âcritical discourse analysis,â âsemantic fieldâ) demonstrating solid conceptual command.
weaknesses: Many paragraphs remain descriptive; quantitative data are not fully interrogated or compared statistically.
Some reliance on popular-science sources limits depth, preventing seamless integration of evidence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed this in the upper band (5â6), but uneven integration of data and descriptive passages warrant a mid-level score. Scaled proportionally to 3/6 within the available total, acknowledging strong source selection and terminology use alongside areas for deeper analytical application.

strengths: Coherent analysis linking linguistic features (modality, metaphor) to geographic facts, producing largely supported conclusions.
Insightful case study evaluations (e.g., Guardianâs framing of sea-level rise) with clear synthesis of figure and text.
weaknesses: Sample size is small and inclusion criteria for article selection are not fully explicit, limiting research rigour.
Critical evaluation of data uncertainty and interdisciplinary synthesis is only partial, reducing incisiveness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Originally placed mid-band (4â6) across the three strands, with research relevance and analysis rated at 5 and argument structure at 4. Scaled to 3/12 in light of the overall mark cap, recognising solid coherence and relevant insights but noting limitations in sample rigour and critical depth.

strengths: Clear academic structure with title page, table of contents, numbered headings, and coherent sectioning.
Relevant visuals are labeled and referenced, aiding comprehension.
weaknesses: Referencing oscillates between Harvard and APA, and some URLs break mid-line, undermining consistency.
Sub-heading hierarchy is inconsistent and figure resolution is low, disrupting text flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 4 (structure), 3 (visuals), and 3 (referencing) suggest solid presentation overall. Scaled to 2/4 to reflect good conventions tempered by formatting inconsistencies and visual quality issues, in line with the examinerâs total mark constraint.

weaknesses: No RPPF reflections provided, resulting in no evidence of reflective thinking.
Absence of personal engagement or intellectual initiative within the submission.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three engagement strands scored zero due to the missing RPPF and lack of reflective or personal commentary. This criterion remains at 0/6, consistent with the examinerâs observations and the requirement to sum to 11.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows a logical structure (impact â media portrayal â comparison â conclusion) and offers critical comments (e.g., âstrategic ritual of objectivityâ).  Yet evaluation of limitations, bias, and methodological reliability is brief, and integration of the two disciplines could be deeper. This places the argument at the lower end of the 4-6 band.

In-text citations (author-date) are present and a full bibliography is supplied, but formatting oscillates between Harvard and APA conventions, line breaks are uneven, and a few URLs appear broken. Consistency is therefore partial, warranting a 3.

The student states a concise title, identifies British media coverage of climate events, and immediately situates the work within the World-Studies theme. The focus (climate depictions in three outlets over three recent UK events) is communicated in both the introduction and the scope section, giving readers a clear understanding of boundaries and purpose. Minor ambiguity remains about whether accuracy will be measured primarily through scientific data or discourse features, preventing a top-band (6) award.

The essay follows a standard academic format with title page, table of contents, numbered headings and clear sectioning.  Minor inconsistencies in sub-heading hierarchy and a very long bibliography list without sub-divisions prevent full polish but still meet the âclear academic structureâ descriptor.

The essay applies factual climate knowledge (sea-level mechanisms, heat-health data, flood economics) to evaluate press coverage and generally links evidence to argument. Nevertheless, many paragraphs remain descriptive; quantitative data are rarely interrogated or compared statistically, so integration is uneven. This aligns with the upper end of the 3-4 band.
No RPPF reflections were provided within the submission; consequently, there is no evidence of reflective thinking.

Analysis connects linguistic choices (e.g., modality, metaphor) to geographic facts and draws reasonable conclusions about accuracy and ideological stance. Evidence is supplied, but cross-comparison is occasionally asserted rather than demonstrated and interdisciplinary synthesis is partial. Thus, analysis is âcoherent with largely supported conclusionsâ (mid-band).

The research question â âTo what extent do editorials from British media present an accurate depiction of climate events?â â is explicitly stated and tightly linked to the chosen case studies. It is neither descriptive nor overly broad, and the phrase âto what extentâ invites analytical judgement.  A small loss of focus occurs because the question does not specify the three hazards or the five-year time frame, so a score of 5 (not 6) is most fitting.

Most research material directly underpins the inquiry: official climate records verify ârealityâ while carefully chosen editorials illustrate representation.  The sample remains small (single article per hazard per outlet) and criteria for inclusion are not fully explicit, limiting rigour but meeting the 4-6 descriptor comfortably.
No evidence of intellectual initiative (e.g., independent data collection, novel methodology) is included in the reflective materials, which are absent, so this strand also receives zero.

The student justifies the dual-disciplinary (Geography + English) design and outlines data sets (official climate statistics) alongside discourse analysis of three nationally read newspapers. Source choice (left/centre/right political spectrum and highest online readership) is explained, demonstrating informed selection.  However, sampling procedures (e.g., number of articles, selection criteria within each outlet) and analytical coding steps are only sketched, so methodology is effective but not fully âcomprehensiveâ, meriting 5.

An extensive bibliography of >80 sources spans government data sets, peer-reviewed papers, NGO reports and the target news articles. All appear directly relevant to either UK climate impacts or media framing, demonstrating deliberate and appropriate selection.  Minor issues of popular-science over-reliance prevent a 6.
Without an RPPF, the examiner cannot identify personal engagement; therefore, the strand scores zero.

Three relevant graphics are inserted, captioned and referenced.  However, figure resolution is low, some captions are generic, and placement disrupts text flow.  The visuals aid comprehension but fall short of exemplary execution, so a score of 3 within the 3-4 band is appropriate.

Key terms such as âenhanced greenhouse effect,â âcritical discourse analysis,â âmodality,â and âsemantic fieldâ are used accurately and consistently across sections. Occasional typographic slips do not undermine the overall depth of conceptual command, positioning the work in the 5-6 band (score 5).

annotations.comment: The introductory paragraphs are descriptive but do not yet link the background to the research question; integrate a roadmap to the case studies for coherence.
The title clearly indicates the studyâs topic but could be more specific by naming the case studies and timeframe, enhancing focus.
The research question is clear but omits the specific five-year period and the three selected hazards; adding these details would sharpen its focus.
The student provides a precise word count under the 4 000-word limit, meeting the formatting requirement.
The table of contents is comprehensive and lists sections with their pages accurately, aiding navigation.
The hierarchy in the table of contents is inconsistent (e.g., some subheadings lack indentation); revise formatting so headings reflect structure.
The student uses terms like âenhanced greenhouse effectâ and chemical formulas accurately, demonstrating strong command of key terminology.
The UN forecast is cited without discussion of uncertainty or error margins; consider evaluating reliability of projections to strengthen analysis.
The use of Hurricane Katrina data effectively illustrates the human and economic impacts of climate-aggravated events.
This paragraph in âImportance of Media Coverageâ is overly long and combines multiple ideas; break into focused sub-paragraphs for clarity.
Citation style shifts (Harvard vs. APA format) and some URLs break mid-line; standardize referencing format consistently.
The scope and rationale section succinctly explains why UK editorials and specific hazards are chosen, strengthening focus.
Methodology outlines data sources but omits sampling criteria and article counts; detail selection steps and sample size for transparency.
The dual-disciplinary framework is well justified, showing informed methodology selection for World Studies integration.
The nexus concept is defined but its role in structuring the geographic analysis is unclear; clarify how the nexus framework guides evaluation.
The language approach lists discourse features but lacks a coding scheme or reliability check; outline categories and procedures for consistency.
The section begins with general mechanisms before the Fairbourne case study; integrating site-specific data earlier would sharpen relevance.
Figure 1âs caption omits axis labels, units, and detailed source information; expand caption to improve clarity and reproducibility.
The integration of figure and text effectively supports the discussion of sea-level rise, demonstrating clear synthesis.
Economic impact figures for coastal flooding are cited without comparative analysis across hazards; add discussion contrasting with other events.
The section title âIncreasing Temperaturesâ is clear and guides the reader effectively through content transitions.
The discussion of biodiversity loss due to warming lacks critical engagement with data limitations; consider evaluating counter-examples or uncertainty.
The analysis of The Guardianâs framing of rising sea levels is insightful, highlighting modality and semantic fields effectively.
The Sun article analysis notes tone but lacks direct quotations; include specific headlines or excerpts to substantiate claims.
The conclusion summarizes findings but doesnât explicitly tie back all components to the research questionâs scope; deepen synthesis to answer it fully.
The bibliography contains a wide range of sources but mixes citation styles; adopt a single referencing convention for consistency.
